sections.header: Verses 1–7 — “Sit in the Dust, Virgin Daughter Babylon”

heb.paragraph: "Go down (yārad), sit in the dust, Virgin Daughter Babylon; sit on the ground without a throne" (v.1). Babylon personified as a dethroned queen forced into slavery. "Take millstones and grind flour; take off your veil. Lift up your skirts, bare your legs, wade through the streams" (v.2). From throne to millstone — the reversal is total. "I was angry with my people and desecrated my inheritance; I gave them into your hand, and you showed them no mercy. Even on the aged you laid a very heavy yoke" (v.6). God used Babylon but Babylon exceeded the mandate: no mercy shown.

notes.note: "Sit in the dust. Take millstones and grind flour." The queen becomes a slave. The most vivid reversal in Second Isaiah.

"I was angry with my people; I gave them into your hand, and you showed them no mercy." God sent Babylon as judgment but Babylon overplayed. The same logic as Assyria in 10:5–15: the instrument exceeds its commission.

refs.note: Babylon's boast 'I sit as queen; I will never mourn' is quoted verbatim and reversed: her plagues come in a single day.
Jeremiah's parallel oracle describes the daughter of Babylon grinding at the mill, enslaved as she enslaved others.
Jerusalem personified as a widow and slave provides the reversed image: now Babylon will suffer what she inflicted.
Nineveh's exposure and shame parallel Babylon's humiliation — empire falls like harlot exposed.

hist.context: Chapter 47 is a taunt song against personified Babylon, the 'virgin daughter' (v.1) who must now sit in the dust, stripped of royal robes, grinding grain like a slave. The reversal is complete: the queen becomes servant, the pampered becomes exposed. YHWH was angry with his people and gave them into Babylon's hand, but Babylon showed no mercy (v.6), and for that she will be judged. The chapter draws on the ancient Near Eastern convention of city-as-woman — Babylon's humiliation is personal and total.

sections.header: Verses 8–15 — “I Am, and There Is None Besides Me”

heb.paragraph: "Disaster (shōʾâ) will come upon you, and you will not know how to conjure it away" (v.11). Babylon says "I am, and there is none besides me" (v.8) — stealing God’s own claim (45:5–6). "I will never be a widow or suffer the loss of children" (v.8). Both will come in a single day (v.9). "You have trusted in your wickedness and said, ‘No one sees me.’ Your wisdom and knowledge mislead you" (v.10). Let the astrologers, stargazers, and monthly prognosticators save you (v.13) — they cannot. They are stubble; the fire burns them (v.14). "Each of them goes on in his error; there is not one that can save you" (v.15). Revelation 18 develops this chapter extensively for eschatological Babylon.

notes.note: "I am, and there is none besides me." Babylon quotes God’s words. The empire claims divine status. "No one sees me" — the delusion of impunity.

"Disaster will come. Keep on with your sorceries! Let your astrologers save you! They are stubble; the fire burns them." The occult arts that made Babylon famous are useless. The most anti-astrology passage in the Bible.
"There is not one that can save you." The final verdict: alone, unsaved, unsaveable. Rev 18:2–19 builds the eschatological Babylon’s fall directly on this chapter.

refs.note: The sudden-destruction motif and the loss of merchants, musicians, and craftsmen echo Isaiah's oracle of swift ruin.
That very night Belshazzar was killed and Babylon fell — historical fulfilment of 'disaster you cannot foresee.'
The earlier Babylon oracle describes the city's desolation — jackals and owls inhabiting the ruins.
Babylon has suddenly fallen and been broken — Jeremiah's parallel prophecy of swift destruction.

hist.context: The taunt continues with mockery of Babylon's astrologers and stargazers (vv.12–15). Let them save you, YHWH taunts — but they are stubble, fire consumes them, and there is not even a coal to warm oneself. The sorceries and enchantments in which Babylon trusted will prove useless. The merchants who trafficked with her will wander off, each to his own quarter, and no one will save her. The historical reference is the fall of Babylon to Cyrus in 539 BC, accomplished (according to Greek sources) without a major battle — the city fell suddenly, as Isaiah promised.

I will take vengeance; I will spare no one.”ESVYour nakedness shall be uncovered, and your disgrace shall be seen. I will take vengeance, and I will spare no one.NIV“Sit in silence, go into darkness, queen city of the Babylonians; no more will you be called queen of kingdoms.ESVSit in silence, and go into darkness, O daughter of the Chaldeans; for you shall no more be called the mistress of kingdoms.

rec.text: ""Sit in the dust. Take millstones and grind flour." The queen becomes a slave. The most vivid reversal in Second Isaiah."

rows.label: Babylon’s Delusion — 47:8–15
rows.text: "I AM, and there is none besides me" — stealing God’s claim. Astrologers CANNOT SAVE. Stubble. Rev 18 develops this.

hebtext.note: "Go down (yārad), sit in the dust, Virgin Daughter Babylon; sit on the ground without a throne" (v.1). Babylon personified as a dethroned queen forced into slavery. "Take millstones and grind flour; take off your veil. Lift up your skirts, bare your legs, wade through the streams" (v.2). From throne to millstone — the reversal is total. "I was angry with my people and desecrated my inheritance; I gave them into your hand, and you showed them no mercy. Even on the aged you laid a very heavy yoke" (v.6). God used Babylon but Babylon exceeded the mandate: no mercy shown.

"Disaster (shōʾâ) will come upon you, and you will not know how to conjure it away" (v.11). Babylon says "I am, and there is none besides me" (v.8) — stealing God’s own claim (45:5–6). "I will never be a widow or suffer the loss of children" (v.8). Both will come in a single day (v.9). "You have trusted in your wickedness and said, ‘No one sees me.’ Your wisdom and knowledge mislead you" (v.10). Let the astrologers, stargazers, and monthly prognosticators save you (v.13) — they cannot. They are stubble; the fire burns them (v.14). "Each of them goes on in his error; there is not one that can save you" (v.15). Revelation 18 develops this chapter extensively for eschatological Babylon.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10: queen to millstone. Justice at 9: Babylon showed no mercy. Rev 18 develops this. The empire that claims to be God is destroyed.

coaching.tip: Babylon is addressed as 'Virgin Daughter' (v. 1) — the same title given to Jerusalem in 37:22. Isaiah uses identical language for both cities, placing oppressor and victim on the same plane. Both are daughters; both face judgment; both are known by God. The parallel humanizes the enemy and warns the reader: what happened to Babylon can happen to any nation that claims ultimacy.

'I am, and there is none besides me' (v. 8). Babylon claims for herself the exact phrase God uses in 45:5 and 46:9. The empire has committed the primal sin: self-deification. Isaiah diagnoses Babylon's fall not as military weakness but as theological plagiarism — she stole God's self-description. Revelation 18:7 echoes this precise phrase in its portrait of fallen Babylon.
